title: CI/CD Pipelines Explained
description:
## Continuous Integration & Continuous Delivery/Deployment

Modern teams ship software in small, frequent increments. **CI/CD** is the practice — and the automation — that makes this safe and repeatable.

### The three terms

| Term | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Continuous Integration (CI)** | Every change pushed to the repo is automatically built and tested, so integration problems surface early instead of at a painful "merge day". |
| **Continuous Delivery (CD)** | Every change that passes CI is automatically packaged into a release-ready artifact. Deploying to production is a **manual button press**. |
| **Continuous Deployment (CD)** | Goes one step further — every change that passes all checks is **automatically released to production**, no human gate. |

### What is a pipeline?

A **pipeline** is an automated, ordered sequence of **stages** that takes source code and turns it into a deployed (or deployable) application. Each stage runs only if the previous one succeeded; a failure stops the line and notifies the team.

### Typical stages

```
  checkout/build  →  test  →  package (artifact / image)  →  deploy
```

| Stage | Typical work |
|---|---|
| **Build** | Check out source, compile, resolve dependencies |
| **Test** | Unit tests, linting, static analysis, integration tests |
| **Package** | Produce a versioned artifact — a `.jar`, npm package, or a container **image** |
| **Deploy** | Push the artifact to a registry/repository and roll it out to an environment |

### A tiny example (GitHub Actions)

```yaml
name: ci
on:
  push:          # run on every push
  pull_request:  # and on every PR

jobs:
  build-test-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4        # build: get the code
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - run: npm test                    # test
      - run: docker build -t app:${{ github.sha }} .   # package
      - run: ./deploy.sh                 # deploy
```

### Triggers

Pipelines start in response to **events**, most commonly:

- **push** — a commit lands on a branch.
- **pull request (PR)** — a contributor proposes a change; CI runs so reviewers see test results before merging.
- **schedule** (cron) or **manual/tag** triggers for releases and nightly jobs.

### Common tools

| Tool | Notes |
|---|---|
| **GitHub Actions** | YAML workflows living in the repo under `.github/workflows/` |
| **GitLab CI** | Defined in `.gitlab-ci.yml`, tightly integrated with GitLab |
| **Jenkins** | Self-hosted, extremely extensible via plugins; pipelines as `Jenkinsfile` |
| **CircleCI** | Cloud-hosted CI/CD configured via `.circleci/config.yml` |

> The goal of CI/CD is **fast feedback and reproducible releases**: every commit is built and tested the same way, every time.
